name: Estadio BBVA
nickname: El Gigante de Acero (The Steel Giant)
tagline: A steel-clad giant with the jagged Cerro de la Silla mountain framed behind one goal — football's most photogenic backdrop.

why.title: The Steel Giant
why.text: A metallic exoskeleton and jagged roofline mirror Monterrey's century as Mexico's steel capital — and echo the peaks of the Sierra Madre.

why.title: The Mountain Shot
why.text: The bowl is built to frame the saddle-shaped Cerro de la Silla behind one goal. Sit on the northwest side for the iconic view.

why.title: Rayados Fortress
why.text: Home of CF Monterrey since 2015, packed with five-time Liga MX champions' fans and the league's loudest derby energy.

why.title: Populous Design
why.text: A $200M Populous build, LEED Silver certified, with 'gills' in the facade that channel airflow to cool the stands.

gettingThere: The stadium sits in suburban Guadalupe at the foot of Cerro de la Silla, about 24 km (15 mi) east of MTY airport. Transit is limited, so plan your route early.
transit: Metrorrey Line 1 east to its terminus (Exposición), then walk through Parque La Pastora to the west gates (~12–15 min).
Uber/DiDi are the reliable default — about 30 min from Centro ($15–20), with surge pricing and pickup zones away from the gates.
Official match-day shuttles are expected from downtown Macroplaza and the Parque Fundidora Fan Festival.
Driving? Pre-buy a parking pass (~MXN 400–700) — Avenida Pablo Livas jams badly on match days.
matchday: The FIFA Fan Festival takes over Parque Fundidora (June 11–July 19) — a 142-hectare former steel foundry with preserved blast furnaces, free entry, giant screens and live music, the perfect setting for the Steel Giant city.
food: Cabrito — roast kid goat, Monterrey's regional signature and a non-negotiable.
Carne asada and arrachera — this is Mexico's grill capital, so eat like it.
Machaca breakfasts — dried beef with eggs, the local morning fuel.
Head to Barrio Antiguo's cobblestone bars or upscale San Pedro for the full spread.

food.name: Azul Restaurant Bar
food.note: Field views; arrachera tacos, octopus tacos, craft cocktails
why: Eat with the pitch in view
Arrachera tacos without leaving the ground

food.name: El Rey del Cabrito
food.where: Centro / Barrio Antiguo, ~25 min from ground
food.note: Legendary cabrito al pastor, the regio non-negotiable
why: Monterrey's most famous cabrito temple
The roast-goat rite every regio swears by

food.name: Los Cabritos
food.note: Closest serious cabrito and carne asada to stadium
why: Nearest real cabrito to the ground
Carne asada in the grill capital

food.name: Pipiripau
food.note: Local-loved cabrito and machaca, old-school market vibe
why: Market-stall machaca locals actually eat
Old-school downtown, zero tourist markup

pre.name: FIFA Fan Festival — Parque Fundidora
pre.where: Parque Fundidora, downtown (~8 km / 5 mi from stadium; match-day shuttles + 8 km fan-walk link the two, or Metro Line 2 to Parque Fundidora)
why: Free official fan zone, giant screens
Live music plus shuttles straight to kickoff

pre.name: Mulligan's Monterrey
pre.where: Barrio Antiguo, downtown (~25 min from ground)
pre.note: Upscale sports bar, golf sim, World Cup crowds
why: Polished base for World Cup crowds
Golf sim while you wait for kickoff

pre.name: The Horde Bar Arcade
pre.note: Arcade-and-beer sports bar, lively pre-match buzz
why: Arcade cabinets plus cold beer
Loud Barrio Antiguo pre-match buzz

pre.name: Botanero Moritas Santa Lucía
pre.note: Cantina with free botanas, screens, beers flowing
why: Free botanas with every round
Old cantina, screens, cheap beer

pre.name: Almacén 42
pre.note: Craft beer hall, 42 Mexican taps, pre-game pints
why: 42 Mexican craft taps to sample
Steps off the Macroplaza fan walk

post.name: Café Iguana
post.note: Iconic rock bar, two stages, rooftop, late nights
why: Monterrey's legendary live-rock institution
Two stages and a late-night rooftop

post.name: Nandas 78
why: Live cumbia bands until 4am
Dance off the final whistle

post.name: NOA NOA Club
post.note: Live bands, dancers, no cover, friendly crowd
why: No cover, friendly mixed crowd
Live bands keep the floor moving

post.name: Strikers Sports Joint
post.where: Av. Eugenio Garza Sada Sur 3680, south city
post.note: US-style sports bar for post-match replays, beers
why: Catch the replays over cold beer
US-style screens for traveling fans

tips: Heat is the #1 issue — Monterrey is among the hottest venues, with afternoons pushing 35–40°C. Hydrate hard and bring electrolytes, a hat and sunscreen.
Target shaded sections under the canopy — coverage is partial and afternoon sun still hits parts of the bowl.
Sit on the west/northwest side so Cerro de la Silla frames the action behind the far goal — the money view.
Allow 2.5+ hours for arrival: congestion, security, and heat-paced walking all add up.
Take Metro Line 1 plus the La Pastora walk, or Uber/DiDi — avoid driving unless you've pre-bought parking.
Base yourself in San Pedro (upscale, safest) or Centro (central, Metro, nightlife); use rideshare over street taxis at night.
weather: Brutally hot in June–July — afternoons routinely hit 35–40°C in a heat-trapping mountain valley, so pack a hat, sunscreen, electrolytes and refillable water access.
lore: Opened August 2, 2015 with the Eusébio Cup — Monterrey beat Benfica 3–0, ending Rayados' 63-year stay at Estadio Tecnológico.
Hosts the fierce Clásico Regiomontano against Tigres UANL, which set the venue's attendance record near 53,000.
The steel facade and mountain-mirroring roofline are a deliberate tribute to Monterrey's industrial steel-and-brewing heritage.
A concert giant too — Coldplay, Bad Bunny, Paul McCartney, Shakira, The Weeknd and Karol G have all played here.

transport.summary: Suburban Guadalupe at the foot of Cerro de la Silla, ~24 km east of MTY airport. Metrorrey Line 1 to its Exposición terminus plus a short walk is the cheapest reliable route; rideshare is the easy default. Transit is limited relative to demand, so go early and build in heat buffer.

rail.name: Metrorrey Line 1 (yellow)
rail.detail: Ride Line 1 east to the end at Exposición, then follow signage over elevated pedestrian bridges / through Parque La Pastora to the gates (~10–15 min walk). From downtown, take Line 2 (green) and transfer to Line 1 at Cuauhtémoc. System is 100% digital — pay with the 'Me Muevo' card or Urbani app QR.

points.t: via bridges and Parque La Pastora.

rail.deal: Metro is the cheapest option (a few pesos); hours extended to ~1–2am for World Cup matches.

bus.name: Metropolitan city buses
bus.from: Av. Pablo Livas / Pino Suárez / Las Torres
bus.detail: Multiple urban routes pass the stadium zone — primary lines cited as 93, 85, 214, 219, 223 (south side near Pablo Livas), plus many feeders from Av. Juárez downtown. Coverage is decent but crowded and confusing for visitors. Low confidence on exact route numbers — verify locally.

shuttle.name: WC2026 match-day shuttles & 8km fan-walk
shuttle.detail: FIFA/host-city match-day shuttles are expected from downtown Macroplaza and the Parque Fundidora Fan Festival. The city has also built an ~8 km pedestrian 'fan walk' linking downtown (via Macroplaza and Fundidora) to the stadium, lined with food stalls, performers and supporter zones. Exact shuttle routes/pricing not yet officially published — treat as provisional.

points.t: routes and pricing not yet published.

transport.fromAirport: Monterrey (MTY) is ~24 km / 15 mi away with no direct rail to the stadium. Easiest is a prepaid airport taxi or Uber/DiDi (~30–40 min, ~MXN 300–600 / $15–35 depending on demand). Transit die-hards can take the Transmetro airport feeder to Line 3, transfer to Line 1 at Félix U. Gómez, and ride to Exposición.
transport.rideshare: Uber and DiDi are the reliable default. There's no single fixed stadium drop-off — drivers use designated zones outside the security perimeter along Av. Pablo Livas (south) and Av. Exposición (north). Expect heavy surge pricing and a walk in from the drop point on match days.
tips: Line 1 to Exposición + the pedestrian-bridge walk is the cheapest dependable route — but it gets packed; go early.
Heading from downtown? You're on Line 2 first, then transfer to Line 1 at Cuauhtémoc — Line 1 alone won't reach you from the center.
The Metro went fully cashless — set up the Urbani app or grab a 'Me Muevo' card before match day, not at a jammed turnstile.
Skip the midday drive in the heat: the ~8 km downtown fan-walk and shaded Parque La Pastora approach are part of the experience, but only if you've hydrated.

parking.summary: About 3,600 on-site spaces across three zones at club games — but for World Cup 2026, general parking at the stadium lots is expected to be prohibited (security perimeter), pushing fans to prepaid satellite lots plus shuttle. Av. Pablo Livas jams badly either way, so arrive 2.5+ hours early or don't drive at all.

lots.name: Zona Sur (Av. Pablo Livas)
lots.detail: Main south corridor along Pablo Livas, closest to most gates and the worst traffic. Higher tier for Clásico Regio derbies.

points.t: main south corridor on Pablo Livas.

lots.name: Zona Norte (Av. Exposición)
lots.detail: North side, closest to the Exposición Metro station — handy if you park-and-walk or get dropped here.

lots.name: Zona Oriente (Av. Nuevo León)
lots.detail: East side, typically the quietest zone on a normal match day.

parking.prepaid: Pre-buy strongly recommended. Rayados club parking is bookable online (via the official ticketing platform / Parkimovil), usually MXN 20–50 cheaper than walk-up. For WC2026, plan on prepaid satellite-lot + shuttle passes sold through the FIFA Ticketing app rather than driving to the venue. Low confidence on final WC2026 parking rules — verify via official FIFA/host-city channels.
parking.accessible: Designated ADA spaces exist in all three zones, with the closest paved spots near gate entrances; wheelchair escorts from parking to accessible seating are available on request.
parking.tailgating: No formal tailgating culture — pre-game energy concentrates in the surrounding lots/streets and at the Parque Fundidora Fan Festival downtown rather than organized car-park gatherings.
tips: Don't drive on a World Cup match day unless you have a confirmed prepaid pass — Av. Pablo Livas gridlocks for hours.
If you must drive to a club game, book Zona Norte and walk in from the Exposición side to dodge the worst Pablo Livas crush.
Confirm WC2026 parking/shuttle rules on the official FIFA app before assuming the on-site lots are open to you.
